description: Data Science interview questions for junior or entry-level candidates.

questions.question: Why is data cleaning important before analysis?

choices.text: Clean data can improve accuracy by reducing errors, missing values, and inconsistencies.
Data cleaning is only for making charts look colorful.
Dirty data always gives better results.
Data cleaning removes the need to understand the business problem.

questions.correctExplanation: Data quality strongly affects analysis quality. Cleaning helps make results more reliable.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Data cleaning is about quality and consistency, not chart colors.
Poor-quality data often leads to misleading results.
Understanding the business problem is still necessary.

questions.question: When might the median be more useful than the mean?

choices.text: When the data has extreme outliers that can strongly affect the average.
When all values are exactly the same.
When you want to delete the dataset.
When the data has no numbers.

questions.correctExplanation: The median is less sensitive to extreme outliers, so it can better represent the center of skewed data.

wrongExplanations.explanation: If all values are the same, mean and median will be the same.
Median is a statistical measure, not a deletion method.
Median requires ordered values, usually numerical or ordinal data.

questions.question: What does the phrase 'correlation does not imply causation' mean?

choices.text: Two variables moving together does not prove that one directly causes the other.
Correlation is always useless.
Causation is easier to prove than correlation.
Two correlated variables must be unrelated.

questions.correctExplanation: Correlation can reveal a relationship, but more evidence is needed to prove a cause-and-effect relationship.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Correlation is useful, but it must be interpreted carefully.
Causation is often harder to prove.
Correlated variables are related statistically, but the relationship may not be causal.

questions.question: Why do data scientists split data into training and test sets?

choices.text: To train a model on one portion and evaluate how well it performs on unseen data.
To make the dataset smaller for no reason.
To hide bad results.
To avoid evaluating the model.

questions.correctExplanation: A test set helps estimate how well a model generalizes to data it has not seen during training.

wrongExplanations.explanation: The split has a purpose: evaluation of generalization.
Splitting data should improve evaluation honesty, not hide results.
The test set is specifically used for evaluation.

questions.question: How should you explain a complex data finding to a non-technical stakeholder?

choices.text: Use plain language, connect the finding to the business question, and explain the impact or recommendation.
Use as many technical terms as possible to sound advanced.
Only show raw code.
Avoid explaining uncertainty or limitations.

questions.correctExplanation: Good data communication focuses on clarity, relevance, and decision-making value.

wrongExplanations.explanation: Too much jargon can confuse non-technical audiences.
Raw code rarely communicates insight clearly to stakeholders.
Limitations and uncertainty are important for responsible interpretation.
